title: What is the K-theory of the Complex Numbers?
speaker: Dustin Clausen
affiliation: Institut des Hautes Études Scientifiques Université Paris-Saclay (IHES)
abstract: I will explain what the question means and how to make it precise. Then I will give a conjectural answer. This is based on joint work with Peter Scholze.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic K-theory and motivic motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls K0 from finitely generated projectives, higher K-groups as homotopy groups of a K-theory space, and the role of algebraic K-theory as a linear-algebraic source of motivic and realization data.

proof_stages.label: The completion problem for C
proof_stages.summary: Ordinary K(C) only sees C as an abstract algebraically closed field, so its higher groups contain wild non-Hausdorff information, while the nontrivial regulator maps suggest a completed or analytic variant capturing topological and cohomological information.

proof_stages.label: Efimov K-theory and rigidification
proof_stages.summary: Naive K-theory of large topological vector-space categories vanishes by an Eilenberg swindle, so the construction is moved to derived categories and Efimov’s extension of K-theory to dualizable presentable stable categories, with tensor rigidification producing the relevant non-compactly-generated categories.

proof_stages.label: Canonical analytic derived category of complex vector spaces
proof_stages.summary: By rigidifying reasonable tensor categories of complete topological complex vector spaces, or equivalently using the liquid analytic-stack formalism for Banach rings, one obtains a canonical analytic derived category of C, concretely described inside a derived category of Hilbert spaces generated by strongly nuclear-dual spaces.

proof_stages.label: Localization and operator-quotient model
proof_stages.summary: Efimov K-theory of a dualizable category is computed as a fibre in a localization sequence, and for the analytic category of C this identifies analytic K(C), up to suspension, with algebraic K-theory of B(H) modulo the rapid-decay Schatten ideal, though the multiplicative structure is obscured in this model.

proof_stages.label: Homotopy-group conjecture for analytic K(C)
proof_stages.summary: The conjectural answer is that analytic K(C) has Z in degree 0 and negative even Tate-twist degrees, C/(2πi)^mZ in positive odd degrees, and zero in positive even degrees, matching the expected Deligne-regulator pattern and Ext groups of filtered complexified abelian groups.

proof_stages.label: Regulator square, evidence, and functorial extensions
proof_stages.summary: Evidence for the conjecture includes the theorem in nonpositive degrees, a natural comparison square with KU and cyclic homology expected to be a pullback, non-Archimedean analogues, extensions to analytic spaces and algebraic varieties with the predicted cohomological functoriality, and companion Hochschild/cyclotomic-trace predictions.

examples.name: Calkin quotient and Schatten-ideal comparisons
examples.note: In response to a question, the known result for B(H) modulo all compact operators, and related Schatten-p work, is contrasted with the rapid-decay ideal to explain why the canonical quotient should see more than KU.
examples.engagement_reason: he concretely identifies the relevant quotient as B(H) modulo a rapid-decay Schatten ideal, uses the swindle to shift K-theory, and compares it with compact-operator and Schatten-p variants

passes.reason: The Calkin and Schatten quotients are related operator-algebraic comparison objects rather than sporadic, degenerate, or adversarial examples.
